summary: Iman is the Islamic concept of faith or belief, centered on inner assent to God, His messengers, and the truths conveyed through revelation. Its precise definition, and its relationship to outward profession and works, became one of the earliest and most consequential debates in Islamic theology.
description:
Iman denotes belief in the truths of Islam, classically framed as belief in God, His angels, His books, His messengers, the Last Day, and (in the standard Sunni formulation) divine decree. Early theologians divided over what iman essentially consists of: whether it is inner assent of the heart (taṣdīq) alone, verbal profession (iqrār), works (ʿamal), or some combination, and whether faith can increase and decrease.

The status of the grave sinner (murtakib al-kabīra) became the crux around which positions on iman crystallized. The Khārijites held that a Muslim who commits a major sin thereby leaves the faith and becomes an unbeliever (kāfir). The Murjiʾa took the opposing view, postponing (irjāʾ) judgment on such a person to God and holding that faith resides in inner conviction and profession, so that even a grave sinner remains a believer whose sin does not nullify iman. The Muʿtazila proposed an intermediate status (al-manzila bayna al-manzilatayn), classifying the grave sinner as neither believer nor unbeliever but as a reprobate (fāsiq). Later Sunni theology, articulated by the Ashʿarī and Māturīdī schools, generally identified the core of iman with inner assent while differing over whether works are constitutive of it, a point on which many Ḥadīth-oriented scholars held that iman comprises belief, speech, and action and admits of increase and decrease.
editorial_notes: This entry is scoped to iman as a theological (kalām) category and its classical definitional dispute; it does not treat the ritual or legal senses of the term or the six articles of faith individually. The positions of the Khārijites, Murjiʾa, Muʿtazila, and later Sunni schools are named but not adjudicated, per the neutrality policy. Attribution of specific formulations (e.g., taṣdīq vs. taṣdīq-plus-works) to named theologians is summarized at a general level; a dedicated entry per school would allow finer sourcing. The claim that later Sunni schools centered iman on taṣdīq is well attested but simplified here. To triangulate the Western academic references with the tradition's own voice (neutrality principle #8), classical primary sources are appended: al-Ashʿarī's heresiographical Maqālāt for the schools' positions and al-Ṭaḥāwī's creed for a Sunni definition of iman; both are rated medium confidence as their relevant passages were not read line-by-line here. Source-quality pass (neutrality principle #9, 2026-07-18): the Encyclopaedia of Islam (Gardet) and Britannica entries were removed as reference works; the entry now rests on one academic monograph (the Cambridge Companion) plus two classical primary texts. Third-lens update (2026-07-18): a modern Muslim scholarly source was appended to add the contemporary-Muslim leg, Sherman A. Jackson's On the Boundaries of Theological Tolerance in Islam: Abū Ḥāmid al-Ghazālī's Fayṣal al-Tafriqa (Oxford University Press / Karachi, 2002), whose study and translation of al-Ghazālī turns on the definition of faith (īmān/islām) and the boundary that separates the believer from the unbeliever, the same definitional axis this entry treats; verified via publisher and library records, PhilPapers, and the Notre Dame Philosophical Reviews notice. The entry now triangulates three lenses that converge on faith as centered on inner assent with a contested outer boundary: Western academic Islamic studies (the Cambridge Companion), classical Muslim primary texts (al-Ashʿarī, al-Ṭaḥāwī), and a contemporary Muslim scholar (Jackson); cited at medium confidence as Jackson's study was not read line-by-line. A dedicated peer-reviewed study focused specifically on the early grave-sinner (murtakib al-kabira) debate and the Murjiʾa or the Muʿtazili manzila bayna al-manzilatayn would still further strengthen the modern-scholarship leg. PARTIAL VERIFICATION (2026-07-19): the six articles of iman were confirmed by directly reading the Hadith of Gabriel (Sahih Muslim 8) and Q 2:285, which are added as high primary-source objects. The entry nonetheless stays reviewed, not verified: its distinctive load-bearing content (the definitional dispute over tasdiq/iqrar/amal and the Kharijite, Murji'a, and Mu'tazila grave-sinner taxonomy) rests on heresiographical and scholarly sources (al-Ashari's Maqalat, al-Tahawi, the Cambridge Companion, Jackson) that are paywalled and not yet read; full promotion to verified awaits reading a load-bearing passage on that dispute.

relations.note: Iman (belief) is defined largely in opposition to kufr (unbelief); the boundary between them is the central question in the grave-sinner debate.
Belief in divine decree (qadar) is included among the objects of faith in the standard Sunni formulation of iman.
The Murjiʾa held that faith persists in the grave sinner and postponed judgment to God.
The Khārijites held that a major sin nullifies faith and renders the sinner an unbeliever.
The Muʿtazila assigned the grave sinner an intermediate status (al-manzila bayna al-manzilatayn).

historical_period.note: The definitional debate emerged in the first Islamic century amid disputes over the community's boundaries and was systematized by the theological schools from roughly the 8th to 10th centuries CE.

sources.citation: The Hadith of Gabriel (Jibril), Sahih Muslim 8 (and Sahih al-Bukhari 50): iman 'is to believe in Allah, His angels, His Books, His Messengers, the Last Day, and to believe in divine decree (qadar), its good and its harm.' The primary basis for the six articles of iman.
sources.license: Primary hadith text; classical Arabic in the public domain. The Hadith of Gabriel read directly and confirmed.

sources.citation: The Qur'an, Q 2:285: 'The Messenger believes in what has been revealed to him from his Lord, and so do the believers. They all believe in Allah, His angels, His Books, and His messengers.' The Qur'anic root of the articles of iman.
sources.license: Primary religious text; classical Arabic in the public domain. Verse read directly and confirmed.

sources.citation: Winter, T. (ed.), The Cambridge Companion to Classical Islamic Theology. Cambridge University Press, 2008.

Jackson, Sherman A. On the Boundaries of Theological Tolerance in Islam: Abū Ḥāmid al-Ghazālī's Fayṣal al-Tafriqa Bayna al-Islām wa-al-Zandaqa. Studies in Islamic Philosophy. Karachi and Oxford: Oxford University Press, 2002. Annotated translation with study by a contemporary Muslim scholar of al-Ghazālī's treatise on the boundary of faith (īmān/islām) and unbelief, bearing on the definition of īmān and who remains within the community of believers.
sources.license: Proprietary; cited for reference, consulted via bibliographic and review records, not read in full.

sources.citation: al-Ashʿarī, Abū al-Ḥasan. Maqālāt al-Islāmiyyīn wa-ikhtilāf al-muṣallīn [The Doctrines of the Muslims and the Divergences of the Worshippers]. Classical heresiography (early 10th c. CE), ed. H. Ritter, Istanbul. Reports the positions of the Murjiʾa, Khārijites, and other groups on the definition of faith (īmān) and the status of the grave sinner.

al-Ṭaḥāwī, Abū Jaʿfar. al-ʿAqīda al-Ṭaḥāwiyya [The Creed of al-Ṭaḥāwī]. Sunni Ḥanafī creed, early 10th c. CE. Affirms that faith (īmān) is assent of the heart and profession of the tongue, addressing the grave-sinner question within the Ahl al-Sunna framework.
